system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 2: PART II - Bossa Nova

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 2 — PART II - Bossa Nova

_Pages 63-86._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Bossa Nova clave definition

> The “clave” is a rhythmic “cell” that’s the root of most rhythmic patterns.

**Significance (p. 61).** Defines the foundational rhythmic concept from which all bossa nova guitar patterns in this chapter are derived.

## Clave inversion rule

> The “clave” can be played as shown above or inverted, but once the

**Significance (p. 61).** States the critical performance rule that clave direction must remain consistent throughout a piece, making reversal a stylistic error.

## Right hand pattern basis

> The “bossa nova” right hand patterns are based on the “clave” figure.

**Significance (p. 62).** Establishes the direct structural link between the clave cell and the guitar right-hand patterns.

## Syncopation vs. thumb technique

> Notice that on the guitar, the syncopation is played on the top voices of the

**Significance (p. 62).** Defines the core right-hand technique split: fingers handle syncopated upper voices while the thumb anchors the beat on bass notes.

## Bass line root-fifth switching

> Like samba patterns, the bass line usually keeps switching between the root

**Significance (p. 62).** Gives the bass-line construction rule and the preferred voicing direction (fifth below root), with the open 6th-string exception.

## Practice method away from guitar

> It’s also a good idea to practice away from the guitar, clapping hands on the

**Significance (p. 62).** Prescribes a body-independence drill that internalises the rhythmic split between bass and upper voices.

## Variation #1 samba relationship

> This variation is the basic pattern with a 16th note syncopation on the Ist

**Significance (p. 63).** Defines Variation #1 technically and establishes the conceptual equivalence of samba and bossa nova patterns.

## Variation #2 extended pattern

> We now have an example of an extended pattern (2 measures long). You can

**Significance (p. 64).** Introduces the concept of a two-measure pattern unit and gives the practical performance context.

## Five-note chord right-hand fingering

> To play the five-note chords in this example, you must use right hand fingers 1,

**Significance (p. 66).** Provides explicit right-hand fingering instruction for dense voicings, extending the three-finger technique to five voices.

## Variation #4 usage context

> This pattern is very useful at the singer’s pause or at the end of a

**Significance (p. 68).** Gives a practical performance-context rule for when Variation #4 is idiomatically appropriate.

## Variation #4 16th-note syncopation option

> You can also syncopate by a 16th note.

**Significance (p. 68).** Introduces a 16th-note displacement sub-variant of Variation #4.

## Harmony change before syncopated note

> Notice that the harmony changes a 16th note

**Significance (p. 69).** States a precise rule about the offset between harmonic rhythm and rhythmic syncopation, a subtle bossa nova detail.

## Variation #3 short-note upper-voice style

> It’s basically the “variation #3”, but using short notes, and playing only the

**Significance (p. 67).** Identifies staccato articulation and registral reduction as a distinct sub-technique applied to an existing variation.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
